mnemonics.title: Mutex Properties
mnemonics.content: SLO: Safety, Liveness, Ordering - Remember as 'Safe LOcking Ensures Order'

mnemonics.title: Ricart-Agrawala Messages
mnemonics.content: REQUEST all, REPLY from all - Remember as 'All Request, All Reply' or AARA

mnemonics.title: Maekawa Quorum Size
mnemonics.content: √N - Remember as 'Square Root gives Quorum' (like square root of requests)

mnemonics.title: Lamport Clock Rules
mnemonics.content: On send: increment. On receive: max + 1. Remember as 'SEND ADD, RECEIVE MAX-ADD'

acronyms.expansion: Ricart-Agrawala - Token-based algorithm using multicast and acknowledgments
acronyms.usage: Used when number of processes is moderate (N < 20)

acronyms.expansion: Critical Section - Code segment accessing shared resources
acronyms.usage: Must be protected by mutual exclusion mechanisms

acronyms.expansion: Lamport Clock - Logical clock for event ordering
acronyms.usage: Establishes partial ordering in distributed systems without synchronized physical clocks

acronyms.expansion: Number of processes in the distributed system
acronyms.usage: Used to calculate message complexity of algorithms

visualTips.visualization: Draw a central circle (process in CS) with arrows coming IN (REQUEST) and arrows going OUT (REPLY). Show N-1 arrows in each direction.
Draw processes as circles in a ring with a small diamond (token) moving clockwise. Highlight that only the diamond-bearer can enter CS.
Show a timeline with events marked as dots. Label each with incrementing numbers. Show causal relationships with arrows.
Draw overlapping circles representing quorums. Show that any two quorums must intersect (the intersection contains a common process).

analogies.analogy: Like a committee meeting room with a 'pass the baton' system - everyone must say 'go ahead' before you can enter.
Like a hotel key card that must be picked up from the front desk and returned before the next person can use it.
Like a shared document with edit timestamps - shows who made changes and in what order, without real-time synchronization.
Like jury selection - you need approval from a diverse group, and any two groups will have at least one common member.
